reasoning: The Lean statement "∀ N, ∃ n > N, ..." is the standard equivalent encoding of "there exist infinitely many n" over the naturals. Using ℕ instead of ℤ for n is harmless: an integer that is a sum of two integer squares is necessarily non-negative, so the informal "infinitely many integers" and the Lean "infinitely many naturals" pick out the same set. Similarly, representing the squares via `i : Fin 6 → ℕ` (six naturals giving three pairs) is equivalent to "sum of squares of two integers" because any integer square equals a natural square (|a|² = a²) and the target values n, n+1, n+2 are non-negative. The three conjoined equations match the three conditions (n, n+1, n+2 each being a sum of two squares) exactly. No hypotheses are added or dropped; the claim is logically equivalent to the informal statement.
